Variant_1.question_text: Your cousin, a successful former student at your school, has just returned from living in another country. Your Principal asks you to write an article for your school magazine about your cousin’s experience. Write your magazine article. You must include the following: • what your cousin achieved at school • where your cousin went and what your cousin did there • why your cousin is a role model for other students. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make your magazine article interesting and informative. Start your magazine article with a suitable headline.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: This question is worth 30 marks in total, with up to 15 marks for Task Fulfilment and up to 15 marks for Language. For Task Fulfilment, assess how well the candidate understands the purpose, addresses the specified situation and audience, uses the appropriate text type, develops all required points in detail, organises given information to support personal opinion, and maintains an appropriate tone and register. Use the following band descriptors: Band 5 (13–15 marks): Very good understanding of purpose; clear awareness of the specified situation and audience; text type entirely appropriate; all required points developed in detail, fully amplified and well organised; given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation; tone and register entirely appropriate. Band 4 (10–12 marks): Good understanding of purpose; an awareness of the specified situation and audience; text type appropriate; all required points addressed not always developed in detail; given information organised to support personal opinion; tone and register appropriate. Band 3 (7–9 marks): Some understanding of purpose; some awareness of the specified situation and audience; text type generally appropriate; at least two required points addressed (both partially or fully developed); given information may not be logically organised to support opinion; tone usually appropriate although there may be slips of register. Band 2 (4–6 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose; some confusion as to the specified situation and audience; text type may be inappropriate; at least one required point addressed (partially or fully developed); given information may be used irrelevantly; tone and register may be uneven. Band 1 (1–3 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose; confusion as to the specified situation and audience; little evidence of the specified text type; none of the required points addressed. For Language, award up to 15 marks based on the accurate use of spelling, punctuation, and grammar, as well as the ability to articulate experience and express what is thought, felt, and imagined (W1), sequence facts, ideas, and opinions (W2), use a range of appropriate vocabulary (W3), and use register appropriate to audience and context (W4). Although specific bands for Language are not detailed here, apply a similar 'best fit' principle, considering W5 for accurate use of spelling, punctuation, and grammar.

Variant_1.question_text: Identify and write down the ways in which sport has developed since ancient times, and the benefits of sport in the present day, as outlined in the passage. USE MATERIAL FROM THE WHOLE PASSAGE. At this stage, you do not need to use your own words. Use note form. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You may find it helpful to use bullet points when listing the content points. You will be awarded up to 12 marks for content points.
Variant_1.marking_scheme:
Award 1 mark for each correct content point identified from the passage, up to a maximum of 12 marks in total. The content points are divided into two sections: The ways in which sport has developed since ancient times and The benefits of sport in the present day. Acceptable answers for each content point are as follows, with synonyms included where applicable:

The ways in which sport has developed since ancient times:
1. People became interested in pushing the human body to its limits (already given as the starting point).
2. Sport began to evolve from the desire for competition, as shown in rock paintings in Libya and 1500-year-old cave paintings in France. Acceptable synonyms for 'desire for competition' include urge for rivalry or need for contests.
3. Sport was formalized, organized, or made into recognizable tournaments.
4. The best, fittest, strongest, or most successful sportsmen were selected, chosen, or picked for military duties, the army, or battle. Acceptable synonyms for 'best/fittest/strongest/most successful' include top, healthiest, most powerful, or victorious; do not accept 'people' or 'men' as substitutes for 'sportsmen'; do not accept 'better/fitter/strong' alone or the lift of lines 8–9 without full context.
5. Training areas were created or made.
6. Sport became an inexpensive pastime, or became available or accessible to all, including both rich and poor. Do not accept 'became more and more accessible alone' or 'became inexpensive alone' or 'non-aristocratic young men' for 'all/rich and poor'.
7. There was the emergence of professional sport or players, or investment in players who are trained to a very high level. Do not accept 'special equipment and clothing alone' or 'athletes/footballers' for 'players'.
8. Sporting events have gone global, worldwide, universal, international, or viral due to technology, or there are more or a larger number of spectators than ever before. Do not accept 'sport/sporting events can be televised and viewed at home alone' or 'many/a lot of spectators alone' or 'large number of spectators alone'.

The benefits of sport in the present day:
9. Playing sport leads to people growing in self-confidence (already given as the starting point).
10. Sport helps people to maintain a healthy weight, lose or shed weight or kilos, or burns up calories through physical exercise. Do not accept examples like cricket, golf, or walking alone.
11. Sport provides multiple, many, or other health benefits, or reduces or lowers the chance or risk of developing illness, disease, or health/medical conditions, such as heart disease. Acceptable synonyms for 'multiple health benefits' include various health advantages; the lift of lines 30–32 must include 'including' or similar expressions like 'such as' or 'for example' to be accepted; do not accept 'prevents illness/disease alone', 'makes/keeps us healthy', or references to specific examples like heart disease alone.
12. Sport produces, creates, or makes endorphins, or produces chemicals in the brain which trigger or cause a positive feeling in the body. Do not accept 'positive feeling alone' or 'chemicals in the brain alone'.

Excess denies credit for additional incorrect information added to a point. Accept lifts from the passage as specified for the points above.

The summary must be in continuous writing and use own words as far as possible; acceptable synonyms for key ideas from 1(a) should be considered, such as 'evolve' for develop or 'health advantages' for benefits.

Band descriptors for content (up to 15 marks):
- Band 1 (13-15 marks): Excellent summary with all content points covered accurately in own words, highly relevant and comprehensive.
- Band 2 (10-12 marks): Good summary with most content points covered in own words, relevant with minor omissions.

- Band 3 (3 marks): Fair organization, some clarity but with inconsistencies.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correct point identified from the passage, up to a maximum of 12 marks. Acceptable answers for benefits: 1. Sense of national pride is generated. 2. Events bring people together from different nations, countries, or places (must include all three: competitors, spectators, and heads of state, unless one or two are presented as an example). 3. Events earn income or wealth for the host country or city. 4. Participants or athletes gain immeasurable satisfaction from being there. 5. Viewing these events raises the profile of sport, raises the importance of keeping fit, or motivates or encourages people to take up or play sport or to keep fit. 6. Famous athletes act as role-models. 7. People see what endurance, work, perseverance, training, or skill can achieve, or witness athletes at the peak of their physical fitness. 8. Events bring extreme enjoyment to spectators or unite spectators in common purpose or friendship. 9. Spectators or people can view the event at home or on television. Acceptable answers for drawbacks: 10. For participants, there is huge pressure to succeed. 11. The mentality, view, opinion, or mindset makes people think that sport is about winning at all costs (may include that this might lead to such a view). 12. Cheating sometimes occurs, even by taking performance-enhancing drugs like steroids (reference to drugs must be presented as an example with words like 'sometimes', 'perhaps', 'even', 'may', or 'might'; awarding both points 11 and 12 if the full phrase from lines 34 is lifted). 13. Huge or enormous sums of money are needed, wasted, or it is expensive to build stadiums or stage or host the events. 14. Budgets, funds, or allocated money for more useful or other things suffer or are taken, or could be used for more useful things. Not accepted responses: For point 2, 'The Olympic Games or the football World Cup bring people together from different towns' or 'bring people together from different nations' alone if not fully qualified; for point 4, lifting lines 11–12 'These events…training (alone)'; for point 5, 'motivates people to join a running club or take out a gym membership (alone)'; for point 6, '(famous) athletes act as role-models for the young (alone)'; for point 7, 'There is nothing more thrilling than witnessing athletes at the peak of their fitness' or 'people see what can be achieved (alone)'; for point 8, lifting 'unites them in a common purpose or friendship (alone)', or 'people' for 'spectators', or 'major sporting events attract spectators (alone)'; for point 12, 'taking performance enhancing drugs (alone)' or 'cheating and/or taking performance enhancing drugs (alone)'; for point 13, lifting lines 38–39 'some people…closing ceremonies'.

Variant_1.question_text: Recently, you were involved in a difficult situation on your way to school. Your best friend was with you and set a good example by helping you. Later, in your English lesson, your teacher asks you to make a speech to your class about the incident. Write your speech. You must include the following: • the name of your friend and where the incident happened • details of the incident and how your friend helped you • how both you and your friend benefited from the event in different ways. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make your speech interesting and informative. Start your speech ‘Good morning, everyone’.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Marking Scheme for Question 1: Up to 15 marks for Task Fulfilment: This assesses how well the candidate articulates experience and expresses what is thought, felt and imagined (fully resolving W1: articulate experience and express thoughts, feelings, and imagination), sequences facts, ideas, and opinions (fully resolving W2: organise facts, ideas, and opinions logically), uses a range of appropriate vocabulary (fully resolving W3: employs varied and suitable words), and uses register appropriate to audience and context (fully resolving W4: adopts language style fitting for a speech to classmates). The candidate must cover all three points in detail: the name of the friend and where the incident happened, details of the incident and how the friend helped, and how both benefited from the event. Award marks based on the following band descriptors: Band 1 (13-15 marks): Excellent task fulfilment with all points covered comprehensively, speech is highly interesting and informative, ideas are well-articulated and sequenced effectively. Band 2 (10-12 marks): Good task fulfilment with most points covered in detail, speech is generally interesting and informative, with reasonable sequencing. Band 3 (7-9 marks): Satisfactory task fulfilment with points covered but lacking detail, speech is somewhat interesting but may lack coherence. Band 4 (4-6 marks): Basic task fulfilment with minimal coverage of points, speech is uninteresting and poorly sequenced. Band 5 (1-3 marks): Poor task fulfilment with little coverage of points, speech lacks interest and structure. Band 6 (0 marks): No relevant content. Up to 15 marks for Language: This assesses accurate use of spelling, punctuation, and grammar (fully resolving W5: correct spelling, punctuation, and grammar usage). Award marks based on the following band descriptors: Band 1 (13-15 marks): Excellent language use with accurate spelling, punctuation, and grammar throughout, varied vocabulary, and appropriate register. Band 2 (10-12 marks): Good language use with mostly accurate spelling, punctuation, and grammar, effective vocabulary. Band 3 (7-9 marks): Satisfactory language use with some accuracy in spelling, punctuation, and grammar, adequate vocabulary. Band 4 (4-6 marks): Basic language use with frequent errors in spelling, punctuation, and grammar, limited vocabulary. Band 5 (1-3 marks): Poor language use with many errors, making meaning unclear. Band 6 (0 marks): No language worth assessing. Total marks: 30, with 15 for task fulfilment and 15 for language.

Variant_1.marking_scheme: For the summary, marks are awarded based on content and style. Band 4 (4–6 marks): Adequate response that addresses most points but lacks detail or persuasion, with basic sequencing. Band 5 (1–3 marks): Weak response that covers few points, lacks organisation or persuasion. Band 6 (0 marks): Inadequate or irrelevant response. Band descriptors for Language: Band 1 (13–15 marks): Excellent use of a wide range of vocabulary and structures, with accurate spelling, punctuation and grammar, and no errors that affect meaning. Variant_1.question_text: Your local council plans to make changes to a public facility in your area, for example a sports centre, a park, a health centre or any other facility. There will be advantages to these changes but some local residents are unhappy about the council’s plans. You decide to write a letter to the editor of a local newspaper to explain how people feel about the possible changes. Write your letter. You must include the following: which public facility it is and the changes the council plans to make; the advantages of the possible changes and why some residents are unhappy about the plans; what you think the local council should do in response to residents’ opinions. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make your letter polite and informative. Start your letter ‘Dear Editor’ and remember to supply a suitable ending.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Marking Scheme for Question 1: Total marks: 30. This question is assessed out of 15 marks for Task Fulfilment and 15 marks for Language. Task Fulfilment (up to 15 marks): Candidates are assessed on how well they follow the task instructions, which include writing a letter that covers all three points in detail: (1) identifying the public facility and the planned changes, (2) explaining the advantages of the changes and reasons why some residents are unhappy, and (3) suggesting what the council should do in response. The letter must be polite and informative, start with ‘Dear Editor’, and have a suitable ending. Award marks based on the following bands: Band 1 (13-15 marks): Excellent response; all three points are covered in detail with relevant ideas, the letter is well-structured, polite, and informative. Band 2 (10-12 marks): Good response; most points are covered with some detail, the letter is generally polite and informative but may have minor omissions. Band 3 (7-9 marks): Satisfactory response; main points are addressed but with limited detail, the letter is somewhat polite but may lack full informativeness. Band 4 (4-6 marks): Weak response; only one or two points are covered superficially, the letter lacks politeness or informativeness. Band 5 (1-3 marks): Poor response; points are barely addressed, the letter is disorganized and not polite. Variant_2.question_text: Identify and write down the problems associated with climate change, and the solutions to the problems associated with climate change, according to the writer. Use material from paragraphs 2 to 7 inclusive. At this stage, you do not need to use your own words. Use note form. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You may find it helpful to use bullet points when listing the content points. You will be awarded up to 12 marks for content points.
Variant_2.marking_scheme:
Award 1 mark for each correct point identified from the passage, up to a maximum of 12 marks in total. The marking is for content points only, and candidates must use material from paragraphs 2 to 7 inclusive. Below are the acceptable answers for problems and solutions, with alternatives where specified. Not accepted responses are listed where applicable.

Problems associated with climate change:
- Acceptable answers: More energy-intensive storms (given point).
- Acceptable answers: Growing or increasing number of wildfires, or more wildfires, or a rise in the occurrence of wildfires. Allow lifting of lines 6–8 from the passage.
- Acceptable answers: Flooding. Excess denies credit if examples are given alone, such as ice melting at N and S Poles, sea levels rising, oceans heating up, or water evaporation falling as rain.
- Acceptable answers: Loss of breeding grounds for some marine creatures, or loss of breeding grounds threatens many ocean species or marine creatures with extinction, or as ocean temperatures rise there is loss of breeding ground for some species or creatures. Not accepted: Threat of extinction of many ocean species alone, loss of breeding grounds alone, ocean temperatures rise alone, or using 'fish' for 'marine creatures'.
- Acceptable answers: Increased number of or more devastating droughts, or the incidence of drought has almost doubled since the early 1990s. Allow lifting of lines 14–15 from the passage.
- Acceptable answers: Periods increase when some diseases can be transmitted or spread, or diseases can be transmitted or spread more often. Allow lifting of lines 18–19 from the passage. Not accepted: Malaria and dengue fever as examples alone.
- Acceptable answers: Increased or more heatwaves cause heat stress or health problems, or record temperatures are symptoms of increased heatwaves. Not accepted: Heat stress, respiratory or cardiovascular illness, or unbearable working conditions as examples alone; or lines 19–20 from the passage alone.
- Acceptable answers: Huge or great financial implications, significance, consequence, pressure, impact, or burden for governments and local communities, or rebuilding homes or entire villages requires a huge amount of finance or money.

Solutions to the problems associated with climate change:
- Acceptable answers: Governments recognise that there is an issue here (given point).
- Acceptable answers: Summit meetings of world, global, or international leaders translate discussion into action, or summits translate discussion into action, or world leaders or world governments make pledges, set targets, take action, or find solutions. Not accepted: Without the idea of world or global; or meetings of world leaders or summits have taken place alone, or world leaders discuss problems alone.

Use your own words as far as possible, but accept lifts where necessary if the meaning is clear.

Marking: Up to 8 marks for content and expression. Band descriptors:
- Band 1 (7–8 marks): Excellent summary; highly relevant, very well organised, easy to follow; excellent use of own words with accurate synonyms; covers all key points clearly.
- Band 2 (5–6 marks): Good summary; relevant, well organised, generally easy to follow; good use of own words with some synonyms; covers most key points.

Band 4 (4-6 marks): Limited style with basic vocabulary and errors. Band 5 (1-3 marks): Poor style with limited vocabulary and errors. Band 6 (0 marks): No attempt or irrelevant. Acceptable answers must include the exact words ‘Stop lying to me. I know you took it!’ in the story.
